topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. My stomach tightened, a familiar, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a request. From Mark. The name alone sent a wave of unpleasantness washing over me. I stared at it, my fingers hovering over the screen as if the glass itself might burn. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, the sound barely audible in the silence of my room. I didn't answer. Instead, I minimized the message, pulling the blanket tighter around my shoulders, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole. The weight of the request felt like another stone added to an already unbearable pile.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone with a deliberate slowness, the screen light illuminating the dull ache behind my eyes. When I saw the profile picture—that familiar smirk, twisted in a way that used to make my skin crawl—my breath hitched. It was him. The impulse to delete the request was immediate, a desperate urge to erase the digital scar. I scrolled through my past, a rapid, unwelcome replay of shared embarrassments and sharp words. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. Why now? What twisted motivation drove him to reach out? I dropped the phone onto the duvet, the impact muffled by the fabric, and let the silence of the room amplify the hollow echo in my chest.

[story 3]

The chime of the incoming request was sharp, cutting through the muted sounds of the evening. I didn't move immediately. I just sat there, rigid, feeling a profound sense of exhaustion settle deep into my bones. Every muscle in my body felt heavy, reluctant to engage with the world. When I finally looked at the notification, the familiar landscape of old antagonism swam before my eyes. A strange, almost paralyzing apathy washed over me, a sort of resignation. I let the notification sit there for several minutes, watching the time tick by with excruciating slowness, before finally tapping the screen to dismiss it, the action feeling monumental and utterly draining.
